person.prominent_name_reason: Col. Roger Hooker Leavitt — Underground Railroad operator. His home in Charlemont MA is on the National Park Service's National Underground Railroad Network to Freedom. Sheltered fugitive slave Basil Dorsey (escaped Frederick County MD 1836) for over five years. Colonel in the Massachusetts militia. Member of Massachusetts legislature. Advocate for the Hoosac Tunnel. Died in Waterloo, Iowa visiting son John Hooker Leavitt, 1885. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roger_Hooker_Leavitt Family correspondence at Library of Congress Manuscript Division.

bio.notes: Named "Hooker" honoring maternal Hooker ancestry through grandmother Sarah Hooker (H00302).

narrative_blocks.header: Basil Dorsey lived five years in hiding
narrative_blocks.body: The fugitive Basil Dorsey, escaped from Maryland in 1836, sheltered more than five years among the Leavitt households at Charlemont — hidden in plain sight while the 1840 census recorded no persons of color there. Brother Joshua wrote from New York: “do all that Christian benevolence requires in his care.”

narrative_blocks.header: The Utica mob converted his parents
narrative_blocks.body: Violence by pro-slavery factions at the October 1835 Utica convention — which he attended with brothers Joshua and Hart — fully converted the elder Leavitts to the cause. Within a year he was president of the Franklin County Anti-Slavery Society.

narrative_blocks.header: Four wives, the last at seventy-six
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed of Keziah Osgood Hunt in 1838, he married her sister Eliza the next year; Olive Warriner in Chicago at seventy-one; and at seventy-six, the schoolteacher Lucy Trowbridge — her first marriage, at fifty-one. He was also among the earliest promoters of the Hoosac Tunnel.

narrative_blocks.body: The fugitive Basil Dorsey, escaped from Maryland in 1836, sheltered more than five years among the Leavitt households at Charlemont — hidden in plain sight while the 1840 census recorded no persons of color there. Brother Joshua wrote from New York: “do all that Christian benevolence requires in his care.”

narrative_blocks.header: The Utica mob converted his parents
narrative_blocks.body: Violence by pro-slavery factions at the October 1835 Utica convention — which he attended with brothers Joshua and Hart — fully converted the elder Leavitts to the cause. Within a year he was president of the Franklin County Anti-Slavery Society.

narrative_blocks.body: Widowed of Keziah Osgood Hunt in 1838, he married her sister Eliza the next year; Olive Warriner in Chicago at seventy-one; and at seventy-six, the schoolteacher Lucy Trowbridge — her first marriage, at fifty-one. He was also among the earliest promoters of the Hoosac Tunnel.

narrative_blocks.header: Dead at thirty-one, three sons left
narrative_blocks.body: She died in November 1838 — the same year Basil Dorsey’s wife died under the family’s protection — leaving John Hooker, William Hunt, and Henry Jenkins, the eldest just seven.

HD0870.prominent_name_reason: John Hooker Leavitt (1831-1906) — Iowa banker, state senator, philanthropist. Pioneer of Waterloo, Iowa 1854; founded Leavitt & Johnson National Bank. Iowa State Senate 1871. Early benefactor of Talladega College, Alabama's oldest HBCU. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Hooker_Leavitt Named "Hooker" for Hooker ancestry.

notable.notable_blurb: Waterloo pioneer banker and Iowa senator

narrative_blocks.header: He surveyed for the Brooklyn Bridge builder
narrative_blocks.body: Trained as a civil engineer, young Leavitt was contracted by John Roebling to survey a large tract — then struck west in 1854 and traded the transit for a Waterloo banking house that bore his name for half a century.

narrative_blocks.header: An early benefactor of Talladega College
narrative_blocks.body: The son of Underground Railroad conductors became an early benefactor of Alabama’s oldest historically Black college, and joined the Pocumtuck Valley Memorial Association where his father had been vice president. In the Iowa Senate he fought James Harlan’s return to Washington.

narrative_blocks.header: She drove a stagecoach at a dead run
narrative_blocks.body: Born to the Illinois frontier, she could ride the wildest colt in the pasture and once drove a four-horse stage at a dead run to catch a train; two Iowa runaway accidents — one that injured her badly — never slowed her.

narrative_blocks.header: A man presided; she ran the school
narrative_blocks.body: Superintendent of Waterloo’s Congregational Sunday school in the late 1860s — when a woman could not properly speak aloud in church — she did all the planning while a male assistant presided, and the school doubled. She taught there more than fifty years.

career.organization: Cedar Falls Trust and Savings Bank / Cedar Falls National Co.
Cedar Valley Building and Loan Association

narrative_blocks.header: He helped found the teachers college
narrative_blocks.body: Arriving in Cedar Falls in 1888, the Beloit graduate helped organize the school that grew into Iowa State Teachers College — today the University of Northern Iowa.

narrative_blocks.header: Trustee of three colleges at once
narrative_blocks.body: He sat as trustee of Grinnell for eighteen years and of Beloit, and a decade as director of the Chicago Theological Seminary — all while running Cedar Falls banks and the Townsend & Merrill lumber treasury. A fall at eighty-eight ended him within a day.

narrative_blocks.header: She married her sister’s widower
narrative_blocks.body: A year after Keziah’s death, Eliza married Roger Hooker and raised her sister’s three boys; she had no children of her own.

narrative_blocks.header: She followed Lucretia Mott in three weeks
narrative_blocks.body: An invalid through her last year, she grieved deeply at the November 1880 death of her “venerable and beloved friend” Lucretia Mott — and, as she had foreseen, followed her within three weeks. Her best years had been spent in Philadelphia as Mrs. H. Ryland Warriner.

narrative_blocks.header: A bride for the first time at fifty-one
narrative_blocks.body: The Buckland schoolteacher — who had started school herself at two and a half — taught from South Deerfield to seven years in the far West before marrying the widowed colonel in 1882, her first marriage.

cross_connections.display_label: was his mother, of Thomas Hooker’s blood
shared his Leavitt descent from Sarah Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: The Liberty Party’s man for Lieutenant Governor
narrative_blocks.body: President of the Franklin County Anti-Slavery Society, he accepted the new Massachusetts Liberty Party’s 1840 nomination for Lieutenant Governor — the abolitionist ticket his son Joshua’s party-building made possible. His household sheltered Basil Dorsey alongside his sons’.

narrative_blocks.header: She petitioned to free the capital
narrative_blocks.body: Chloe circulated a petition demanding the abolition of slavery in the District of Columbia while her husband ran for Lieutenant Governor on the Liberty ticket and her sons ran the Underground Railroad.

sources.short: Emerson Davis, Biographical Sketches of Congregational Pastors

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1758 minister at Walpole and Charlemont
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1758 and was ordained at Walpole, New Hampshire on May 27, 1761. After 1767 he served the Charlemont, Massachusetts pulpit until 1785.

narrative_blocks.header: Uncle of Chief Justice Oliver Ellsworth
narrative_blocks.body: His sister Jemima Leavitt married Capt. David Ellsworth, making him the uncle of Oliver Ellsworth — Founding Father, signer of the Constitution, and third Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court. The Ellsworth and Leavitt households were closely tied through Suffield, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Dragged a runaway enslaved woman home by rope
narrative_blocks.body: In 1764 he was dismissed from his Walpole ministry after allegedly dragging a runaway enslaved woman home behind his horse by a rope tied to his saddle. He was installed at Charlemont, Massachusetts three years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Dismissed from Charlemont for his Loyalist sentiments
narrative_blocks.body: He was dismissed from his Charlemont, Massachusetts ministry in 1785 for Loyalist sympathies during the Revolution. He preached on alone in a schoolhouse and sued his former parishioners for back wages, winning roughly £700.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died from medicine her daughter gave her
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Sarah Hooker Leavitt died in October 1791 at age 49 'as a consequence of taking wrong medicine' given to her by their daughter Clarissa. The obituary called the loss sudden and unexpected.

narrative_blocks.header: Two grandsons defended him in a 1904 book
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his grandsons John Hooker Leavitt and William Hooker Leavitt published a 1904 volume titled 'A Sketch of the Life and Character of Rev. Jonathan Leavitt.' They tried to portray their controversial ancestor as a misunderstood and complicated man.

education.notes: B.A. — Yale 1758. Tier-2 spouse of Sarah Hooker (H00302). Two brothers also Yale graduates. Nephew-by-marriage to Chief Justice Oliver Ellsworth.

cross_connections.display_label: Her descendant designed USS Hartford's steam engine
Her grandson sheltered fugitive Basil Dorsey five years

bio.notes: Sarah No. 2 — replacement naming after first Sarah (H00301) who died April 1741

narrative_blocks.header: Married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt of Heath
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt and moved to Heath, Massachusetts. Leavitt was twice dismissed from churches — once for dragging a runaway enslaved woman home by rope, and once for Loyalist sympathies.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children — eleven sons and one daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She had twelve children with Jonathan Leavitt — eleven sons and one daughter named Clarissa. EH lists only three; other sources confirm the full count of twelve.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter gave her wrong medicine, killing her
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly in 1791 after her daughter Clarissa gave her the wrong medicine. Clarissa herself died unmarried seven years later — never recovering from the accident.

narrative_blocks.header: Underground Railroad station owners descended from her
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her grandsons — Roger Hooker Leavitt and Hart Leavitt — operated Underground Railroad stations in Charlemont, Massachusetts, sheltering the fugitive Basil Dorsey for five years. Roger's home is on the National Park Service Underground Railroad Network to Freedom.

narrative_blocks.header: A wrong medicine, given by her daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly at Heath on October 11, 1791, after taking the wrong medicine — handed to her, the Yale record says, by her daughter Clarissa. The funeral text was “He hath done all things well”; the Connecticut Courant carried the notice.
